messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] image with R-devel and gcc-16
messages.body_plain:
On 1 June 2026 at 11:09, Brad Eck wrote:
| I received a note that my package -- epanet2toolkit -- was showing a
| warning in the debian-gcc results on CRAN.
| https://cran.r-project.org/web/checks/check_results_epanet2toolkit.html
| 
| I'd like to reproduce the warning and fix it.
| 
| Is anyone aware of a docker image with this setup?
| 
| When I pull rocker/r-devel:latest, I see gcc-15. Same with debian:testing.

The _default compiler_ in Debian changes more slowly than the _available
compilers_. But you were very close. Use either one of

  rocker/r-base
  r-base         (same as previous one, but under different 'official' handle)

for the default container with R 'release', or as you did

  rocker/r-devel

to get a weekly build of R-devel on top of it (and remember to use RD for for
R-devel, R is still 'R-releasse' in it) and then do

  apt update -q
  apt install gcc-16

(followed by as-needed the gfortran and g++ versions if your package need
them) and do the usual bit via the compiler variable in your src/Makevars, or
a ~/.R/Makevars, to make that compiler the default.  Come to r-sig-debian if
you have more 'Debian-specific' questions on the process.

I have used this approach a few times myself for such tasks. 

Hope this helps,  Dirk (who maintains these containers)

-- 
Dirk Eddelbuettel | edd at debian.org | http://dirk.eddelbuettel.com

I always forget about this part but I just needed it myself (!!) so for that
debugging task twenty minutes ago I did

    mkdir ~/.R
    $EDIT ~/.R/Makevars

inside the container (installing `mg` as a lightweight version with the
keybindings of The One True Editor (TM), I sometimes pick `vim-tiny` too) to
set CXX20=g++-16 which was what I needed right now. My need was also for
g++-16 which seemingly tightens again what headers are auto-included, and did
not involve R-devel.

Dirk

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] DESCRIPTION meta-information, but only for R-devel-Debian-GCC
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] DESCRIPTION meta-information, but only for R-devel-Debian-GCC
messages.body_plain:
Hi everyone,

I see that R package 'tinycodet' v 0.6.2 (this version has been up for several months now) suddenly has an error, but only for `r-devel-linux-x86_64-debian-gcc`. The package fails at the check "DESCRIPTION meta-information". CRAN has not provided any additional information.

It's odd that the DESCRIPTION file is considered bad for Debian-gcc, but not for all other operating systems. If there's a mistake in the DESCRIPTION file, I'd expect there to be an error for every OS.

Link to package: https://cran.r-project.org/package=tinycodet

I wanted to update my package (update is already on GitHub), but I need to know why this fail happens before I can update my package.

Thanks in advance for any help/info!

Kind regards,

Tony

messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] DESCRIPTION meta-information, but only for R-devel-Debian-GCC
messages.body_plain:
On 2026-06-07 2:09 p.m., Tony Wilkes wrote:
> Hi everyone,
> 
> I see that R package 'tinycodet' v 0.6.2 (this version has been up for several months now) suddenly has an error, but only for `r-devel-linux-x86_64-debian-gcc`. The package fails at the check "DESCRIPTION meta-information". CRAN has not provided any additional information.
> 
> It's odd that the DESCRIPTION file is considered bad for Debian-gcc, but not for all other operating systems. If there's a mistake in the DESCRIPTION file, I'd expect there to be an error for every OS.
> 
> Link to package: https://cran.r-project.org/package=tinycodet
> 
> I wanted to update my package (update is already on GitHub), but I need to know why this fail happens before I can update my package.
> 
> Thanks in advance for any help/info!
> 

It's not very unusual to have a failure on only one system, or only a 
few.  It could be that there's a new test that has been added and it has 
only been deployed on one R-devel system so far, or the error really 
does only affect one platform.

However, the `tinycodet` example you posted does look like there's an 
issue with the test machine.  It doesn't give complete test results, it 
appears to have failed during the test:  we see "checking DESCRIPTION 
meta-information ..." but don't see "OK" or "FAIL", that's the end of 
the display.

So I think if you don't see a comment here about the issue in the next 
day or two, it would be reasonable to write to CRAN to ask what 
happened, and what you need to do to fix it.

Duncan Murdoch

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Advice on dependencies
messages.body_plain:
Dear package developers, dear CRAN team,

I hope you find my problem interesting: I have two packages, rswipl and
rolog, both enable to interface R with SWI-Prolog. rswipl packages the
entire SWI-Prolog-sources (except for a few dependencies), rolog is meant to
be lightweight and tries to connect either to rswipl or to an existing
installation of SWI-Prolog on the system. Now, SWI-Prolog is not available
on the CRAN testing machines, so rswipl needs to be added as a dependency
("LinkingTo") to rolog.

Of course, this implies that whenever rolog is installed, rswipl is also
installed, which is against rolog's spirit (it should connect to SWI-Prolog
and only fall back to rswipl if SWI-Prolog is missing). I tried to move
rswipl from "LinkingTo" to "Suggests", but this only works on some machines:

https://cran.r-project.org/web/checks/check_results_rolog.html

(Please ignore the "musl" problem shown below, that is unrelated and has
been fixed)

Can I do something about this? Some, but not all, CRAN machines seem to
install rswipl automatically, even though it's _just_ mentioned in Suggests,
others don't. Your thoughts are much appreciated. If everything fails, I can
revert the dependencies.

Best wishes,

Matthias

| be lightweight and tries to connect either to rswipl or to an existing
| installation of SWI-Prolog on the system. Now, SWI-Prolog is not available
| on the CRAN testing machines, so rswipl needs to be added as a dependency
| ("LinkingTo") to rolog.
|
| Of course, this implies that whenever rolog is installed, rswipl is also
| installed, which is against rolog's spirit (it should connect to SWI-Prolog
| and only fall back to rswipl if SWI-Prolog is missing). I tried to move
| rswipl from "LinkingTo" to "Suggests", but this only works on some machines:
| 
| https://cran.r-project.org/web/checks/check_results_rolog.html
| 
| (Please ignore the "musl" problem shown below, that is unrelated and has
| been fixed)
| 
| Can I do something about this? Some, but not all, CRAN machines seem to
| install rswipl automatically, even though it's _just_ mentioned in Suggests,
| others don't. Your thoughts are much appreciated. If everything fails, I can
| revert the dependencies.

I have pointed out in the past that R / CRAN conflate 'build-time' and
'run-time' dependencies by giving us only a single 'depends' (using the term
here as a catch-all for what CRAN calls 'strong dependencies', ie Imports,
Depends, or LinkingTo).

That leads to situations where eg my BH package with its footprint of 155 MB
ends up being installed even though it was only needed at compile-time /
build-time. This is somewhat suboptimal, but it is also I can do here.

You are in a similar situation.

There is a related one. Sometimes we have build-dependencies that can only
exist on some platforms. Other platforms nag, or signal a fail. Which gets
old after a while. So one can protect against available / not-available with
appropriate pre-processor macros. I have done so for some of my packages (two
that I can think of right now) and you could do the same: then rolog would
always "build" but of course only be functional if rswipl, or a full
SWI-Prolog, were found at build-time so that the #if turns to true,
 
So in short, we once again play the hand we are dealt.

At CRAN our choices as package developers are limited; on the other hand what
builds generally does so predictably. It is what it is: generally quite good,
but could be improved. But given that CRAN has historically been averse to
delegating or changing things, improvements may not be exactly around the
corner. Proving binaries via r-universe is possible alternative.

Cheers, Dirk

--

? Mon, 22 Jun 2026 19:48:47 -0700
Josiah Parry <josiah.parry at gmail.com> ?????:

> I believe that R6 is only needed at build time and not as an
> actual dependency.

While the environment chain of an R6 class does include the R6 package
namespace, all the important bits of the R6 package live in the
enclosed non-namespace environment that is automatically serialised
together with the class:

https://github.com/r-lib/R6/blob/main/R/aaa.R

R6::R6Class() |> parent.env() |> parent.env() |>
identical(loadNamespace('R6'))
# [1] TRUE

but:

Rscript -e '
 R6::R6Class("foo", list(bar = \() "baz")) |> saveRDS("foo.rds")
'
R CMD REMOVE R6
Rscript -e '"foo.rds" |> readRDS() |> parent.env()'
# <environment: 0x557cc5db1070>
# attr(,"name")
# [1] "R6_capsule" # <-- R6 mostly lives here
Rscript -e 'readRDS("foo.rds")$new()$bar()' # class works regardless
# [1] "baz"

When R CMD INSTALL evaluates the source code of the package, it
similarly saves the contents of the resulting environment to the
lazy-load database. The resulting binary package doesn't have to
require R6 to be installed.

I think that the R6 developers could make the class objects even more
"officially" independent from the R6 namespace making the capsule
environment enclosed by the base namespace instead of the package
namespace.

> I think creating a canonical distinction for these would be a net
> positive.

When a binary package has less dependencies than the corresponding
source package, it's annoying to install the extra dependencies, but at
least nothing breaks. Unfortunately, it's also possible for a binary
package to have more strict dependencies than the corresponding source
package. For example, a package missing an S4 class import can copy the
class to its own namespace, including references to private objects
that can later be broken [1]. A package that offers a C-callable
interface could undergo a source-compatible but binary-incompatible
change [2]. There are probably more ways in which a binary package
could break despite the source package still being compatible, which is
why R now checks the build dates in old.packages() [3]. (See also SVN
r89371 by Simon Urbanek.) Some of these binary dependencies can
probably be determined automatically (? la ${shlibs:Depends} in
Debian?), others will require work from the maintainer (similar to how
Matrix::Matrix.Version()[["abi"]] works), but how to declare the
absence of binary dependency?

Mike also mentions build-time dependencies: are those for R CMD INSTALL
--build, or R CMD build?

-- 
Best regards,
Ivan

[1]
https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2025-January/083779.html

[2]
https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-package-devel/2025q1/011376.html

[3]
https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-help/2026-June/481694.html
It's a shame that the post by Jinsong Zhao didn't reach the mailing
list.

My +1 as well.

More generally, I think there is need to distinguish packages that are required by a given package, and that need to be loaded or attached when the given package is; versus those that do *not* need to be loaded into RAM when the given package is attached.

I have one such example in a package under development. My way to handle this situation is to list the dependency in the "Imports:" field in DESCRIPTION, but not to list it in any `import()` or `importFrom()` in the NAMESPACE. I doubt CRAN will accept that, but I'll try to explain the reasons for doing so.

Luca

I think that is the accepted solution.  I think WRE doesn't say this 
explicitly, but it says:

"The ?Imports? field should not contain packages which are not imported 
from (via the NAMESPACE file or :: or ::: operators), as all the 
packages listed in that field need to be installed for the current 
package to be installed. (This is checked by R CMD check.)".

To me this implies the package doesn't need to be mentioned in the 
NAMESPACE file.  I think the manuals don't specify when a package like 
that will be loaded, but I think currently it will be loaded on first 
use, not when the package declaring its import is loaded.

Duncan Murdoch

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Assumed-size arrays in fortran and memory sanitizer
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Assumed-size arrays in fortran and memory sanitizer
messages.body_plain:
Hi all,

I am the maintainer of an R-package, called limSolve, that solves least-squares problems. The package does not survive the clang-ASAN compilation, and is now scheduled to be removed from CRAN.

The error I get is (simplified):
ERROR: AddressSanitizer: memcpy-param-overlap: memory ranges [...) and [..) overlap.

I have tried to make fixes to the exact location where this error emerges, but, even if I am successful, then a similar error emerges somewhere else.

After trying many things, my best guess now is that this is due to the use of *assumed-size arrays* in this old fortran code, which may not be compatible with the address sanitizer.

As a simple example of what I think is happening:
in the function *DLSEI* a matrix called *W* is defined as:
                double precision :: W(MDA, *)
This means that W has MDA rows, and an undefined number of columns (but, as the exact size of this matrix is set in R, its memory location is known).
Now several functions are called that operate on *rows* of W.  Let?s say, somewhere it calls a function *FUN*:
                call FUN ( W(5,1), ...)
which would operate on row 5 of W.

The problem, I think, is that, within FUN, W is also defined as:
                 double precision :: W(MDA, *)
But, as in the calling function (DLSEI), the address is passed of the 5th element in W (the first column of row 5), and not of the first element, this means that the array W in FUN extends 5 elements beyond the memory that is actually allocated to it.
My guess is that the address sanitizer finds that this matrix overlaps with some other matrix or vector, hence the error?

I am wondering whether this *is* an error in this code, as the memory extending beyond the original memory will not be altered (although I cannot be sure of this).

This is not something that is easily fixed, also given the large number of such constructs in this fortran code, and the complexity of the code.
My question is then: how am I supposed to deal with this? If I abandon this package, then some dependencies will also need to be abandoned or alternatives will need to be found. So if I can avoid this I would definitely prefer this.
I realise this is not a very simple problem, but I would be grateful for any suggestions that people might have,

Thanks,
Karline

messages.body_plain:
Karline,

as far as I can tell, the issue (assuming we're talking about  https://www.stats.ox.ac.uk/pub/bdr/memtests/clang-ASAN/limSolve/00check.log ) looks like a compiler optimisation issue where the compiler tries to optimise the copy (since the xDCOPY code does it one element at at a time for subsequent entries in the same column) by using memcpy() of the region (the output shows 32 bytes so 4 elements are being copied), but that is illegal for overlapping regions - which is the case here. The Fortran code is specifying the copy to be done sequentially (it is a element-wise loop) so the algorithm works fine as long as the second (target) region comes before the first region (which is it true as the code guarantees it) so the compiler should not be attempting to use memcpy() to do that, but at best should use memmove() in that case. The problem is that the resulting code will actually be wrong, so ASAN is correct, but it's not the Fortran's code fault. I'd try to reduce optimisations to see if that problem goes away. Unfortunately, I could not find anything on the optimisation in flang nor if there is a way to disable it in the code itself. Probably best would be to report it and see what the flang authors think (I could be wrong).

Cheers,
Simon

messages.body_plain:
? Thu, 25 Jun 2026 12:00:22 +0000
Karline Soetaert <Karline.Soetaert at nioz.nl> ?????:

> The error I get is (simplified):
> ERROR: AddressSanitizer: memcpy-param-overlap: memory ranges [...)
> and [..) overlap.

In example(RigaWeb), the line

CALL xDCOPY (M-ME, W(ME+1,J), 1, W(KRANKE+1,J), 1)

is evaluated for

M-ME = 46
ME+1 = 15
KRANKE+1 = 14
NP1 = 27

That is, the local copy of the BLAS DCOPY() subroutine is asked to copy
46 elements between W(15:60,J) and W(14:59,J) for all J in 1:27. These
regions overlap. I think this violates the Fortran standard on aliasing
between dummy arguments [*], because the same part of the vector is
accessible through different arguments to xDCOPY(). 

I'm afraid you'll have to create a different subroutine, say, xDMOVE,
to move elements of the same column, and use it in the two places where
regions of the same array are currently given to xDCOPY.

-- 
Best regards,
Ivan

[*] See <https://j3-fortran.org/doc/year/18/18-007r1.pdf> for the 2018
draft, in particular "NOTE 2" at page 313 about partial overlap.

messages.body_plain:
Dear Karline,

I had (very) similar problems with my MSCMT package, which is hardly 
surprising, as I had (with your kind permission) adopted parts of the 
Fortran code from the limSolve package years ago.
My workaround involved enlarging a WORK array so that overlapping copies 
could be replaced by non-overlapping ones (and adapting the surrounding 
code accordingly).
The changes that worked for me can be found here:
https://github.com/mabe0033/MSCMT/commit/6241141e616d71d15c7b3ca62e4c8a2e7c9be62b

I hope this helps you to resolve the issues in the limSolve package. If 
you have any questions about this or if there?s anything else I can do 
to help, please do get in touch.

Best,
Martin

messages.body_plain:
Hello.  I submitted an update to the oce package a couple of hours ago, and got an email (pasted below a line of three dashes, below) that states that it fails pretests.  I've followed each of the links, and cannot see what the problem is, *except* for the test taking 16 minutes, in excess of 10 minutes.

This package has been on CRAN for maybe a decade (times flies faster as I grow older) and it has taken over 10 minutes to build for several years.  I think I once determined that even removing all the documentation and tests would still have it over 10 minutes, although I can't quite remember how I determined that.  Plus, I think documentation and tests are a big advantage of CRAN over, for example, Python repositories.

I had noted the time issue in my cran-comments.md file, stating near the start: "As in previous CRAN submissions, we request an exception to the 10-minute rule, on account of the package size (300+ functions, covering 60k lines of R, 6k lines of C/C++ and 1k line of Fortran)."

Is there another action I ought to undertake?

Thanks.  Oh, and happy day-before-Canada-day.

Dan E. Kelley [he/him/his 314ppm]
Professor, Department of Oceanography
Dalhousie University
PO BOX 15000
Halifax, NS, Canada B3H 4R2
Dan.Kelley at Dal.CA

Hi Dan:

I am no expert on this,  but the automated pretests are just that,  and they have certain built-in rules that they check for,  and if one is violated you get a note.  Each time you submit it will take a human to look and decide to waive the requirement,  so if you have responded as they suggest I would say just hold on for a day or two for a real person to  look at that.  I have a package whose examples always where just over the limit and I had to go through the same process.   The main thing is there are only a limited number of people who can waive the requirement and they are pretty busy.

And since I work in related areas,  ?oce? is a great package and should be allowed.

-Roy
